name: Futsu-no-Mitama
description: Futsu-no-Mitama, also known as Futsunomitama-no-Ōkami, is a sacred sword enshrined at Isonokami Futsu-Mitama Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan. It is considered one of the three great swords of Japan, alongside Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi and Totsuka-no-Tsurugi. According to legend, the sword was wielded by Takemikazuchi, the god of thunder and swords, during the subjugation of the Middle Land (Ashihara no Nakatsukuni). It played a crucial role in quelling rebellious deities and establishing control over the land, ultimately paving the way for Ninigi-no-Mikoto, the grandson of Amaterasu, to descend and rule. The Futsu-no-Mitama is believed to possess immense spiritual power and is revered as a symbol of divine authority, martial prowess, and the establishment of imperial rule. Its presence at Isonokami Shrine makes the shrine a significant site for warriors and those seeking protection. The sword is not only a physical object but also a powerful symbol deeply intertwined with the origins and legitimacy of the Japanese imperial line. It represents the divine mandate to rule and the strength necessary to maintain order and prosperity. Its very name, Futsu, alludes to the sound of cutting, signifying its effectiveness in vanquishing evil and clearing obstacles.
subtitle: Divine sword of thunder and subjugation
origin: Wielded by Takemikazuchi during the subjugation of the Middle Land
currentLocation: Isonokami Futsu-Mitama Shrine, Nara Prefecture, Japan

keyMyths.title: Subjugation of the Middle Land
keyMyths.description: Takemikazuchi used Futsu-no-Mitama to subdue rebellious deities, establishing order and paving the way for the descent of Ninigi-no-Mikoto. This event is crucial to the foundation myths of Japan.

keyMyths.title: Enshrinement at Isonokami
keyMyths.description: After its use in the subjugation, the sword was enshrined at Isonokami Shrine, becoming a symbol of imperial power and divine protection. The shrine is a significant location for warriors and those seeking spiritual strength.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: Translation and annotation of the Kojiki, a primary source for Japanese mythology.
sources.text: Translation and annotation of the Kojiki, a primary source for Japanese mythology.

sources.author: W.G. Aston
